payload.text: Create an infinite choose-your-own-adventure engine. It should be a text-based game where my choices genuinely alter the upcoming plot, not just lead to pre-set paths. Use a consistent art style for real-time generated images so characters look the same throughout the journey. Feature a dynamic sidebar that tracks my "Inventory" and "Current Quest," which the AI must update automatically as I encounter new items or characters in the story.

**Analyzing the Requirements**

I'm currently dissecting the user's needs for this "Infinite Choose-Your-Own-Adventure Engine." I've pinpointed key elements: a text-based, choice-driven structure with a generative plot, and a consistent art style. I'm also considering the user interface, especially the need for a dynamic sidebar and intuitive navigation.


**Prioritizing AI Integration**

I've been exploring the integration of AI models, particularly focusing on Gemini's capabilities. I'm prioritizing the use of `gemini-3-pro-image-preview` for image generation, offering users various size options. For the chatbot/game logic, `gemini-3-pro-preview` seems like the best fit, while `gemini-2.5-flash-lite` might be suitable for low-latency tasks, though I'm carefully considering how to best balance response speed with the need for high-quality, consistent game logic. I need to prioritize the prompt's request for "Fast AI responses" but also the need for quality story generation and inventory management, meaning I'll likely use Gemini Pro for the main game logic and Flash Lite for quick interactions or checks.


**Balancing AI Models**

I'm now prioritizing `gemini-3-pro-preview` for complex story generation and game logic due to its superior reasoning capabilities, especially for inventory and quest tracking, despite the initial emphasis on speed. `Gemini-2.5-flash-lite` might be reserved for faster, less critical functions like a "Quick Chat" side feature or perhaps initial prompt expansions if I decide to add them. The core game engine will lean heavily on `gemini-3-pro-preview` to balance prompt requests.


**Deciding Model Usage**

I'm now solidifying my model selection. Given the project's complexity and the need for robust inventory management, I've decided to prioritize `gemini-3-pro-preview` as the core game master, handling story generation, inventory tracking, and overall game logic. `Gemini-3-pro-image-preview` will handle the visual generation as requested. I've re-evaluated `gemini-2.5-flash-lite`, and now plan to use it for generating the choices and the initial summaries, since the game logic needs to be the more complex, quality version.


**Constructing the Game Engine**

I'm now deeply engrossed in structuring the game's core architecture.  `gemini-3-pro-preview` will manage all game logic, driven by a defined state: history, inventory, quest, loading flags, current image, and API key.  My components will include a layout with a sidebar for inventory and quest display, a story feed for narrative display, an action area for user input and choices, and an image generator.  The `gameMaster.ts` service will handle Gemini interaction, requesting new narrative segments, inventory and quest updates, visual descriptions, and choices, ensuring `gemini-3-pro-preview` drives the "genuinely altering plot" that users will love.


**Prioritizing Speed and Quality**

I'm now focusing on balancing the conflicting demands for fast AI responses and high-quality game logic.  I realize that the initial focus on `gemini-3-pro-preview` for game mastering may need to be adjusted to accommodate `gemini-2.5-flash-lite`'s speed. I'm considering using `gemini-2.5-flash-lite` for the rapid-response aspects, but maintaining `gemini-3-pro-preview` for complex story generation to provide quality gameplay. The optimal strategy may be to delegate rapid tasks to the smaller model while ensuring the more complex logic is handled by the "game master". I'll also try to combine the files into a single prompt for Gemini to increase efficiency.


**Deciding on Model Usage**

I've decided to prioritize a single model, `gemini-3-pro-preview`, for both story generation, game logic (inventory, quests), and text-based choices, given the project's complexity and the crucial need for seamless integration. The image generation, as requested, will still be handled by `gemini-3-pro-image-preview`. I am forgoing the plan to use flash lite to maintain coherency. The quality and coherence in the inventory and quest system is paramount. I will default to Pro.


**Fine-tuning the Game Engine**

I'm now fully focused on the implementation details. I've designed the `GeminiService` to call the `gemini-3-pro-preview` model for story generation and game logic, returning structured JSON data. It uses the `gemini-3-pro-image-preview` for image generation, including size control. I've decided to prioritize a "dark mode" UI for a classic RPG vibe. I am still avoiding adding the "thinkingConfig", and defaulting to standard generation to reduce latency.


**Addressing Speed and Mode**

I'm now contemplating how to balance fast AI responses with high-quality gameplay. The initial plan of defaulting to the Pro model may change: I'll now design a system where users can switch between a "Fast Mode" with the Flash Lite model and a "Quality Mode" with Gemini 3 Pro. The goal is to provide users with a customizable experience that meets both needs. The model selector will default to Pro.


**Integrating User Image Preferences**

I'm incorporating the user's requested "user-specified image size" into the core architecture. I'll add a dropdown or settings gear, allowing users to select their preferred image resolution (1K, 2K, or 4K) for a customizable experience. My service will handle `gemini-3-pro-image-preview` requests, and the audio will be omitted to favor text and images.


**Refining Model Integration**

I'm now fully committed to the model architecture. Given the need for high-quality inventory and quest tracking, I'll leverage `gemini-3-pro-preview` as the core text model for both modes, with `gemini-3-pro-image-preview` handling image generation. The focus will be on optimizing the prompting and architecture to maximize speed in the quality mode. I am now adding file descriptions and structure and will be implementing this architecture.


**Adjusting the Model Strategy**

I'm now implementing the model architecture. I'll use `gemini-3-pro-preview` as the core text generator for "Quality Mode" and `gemini-2.5-flash-lite` for "Fast Mode". I'll use `gemini-3-pro-image-preview` for image generation, with customizable sizes. The response schema will be JSON for structured data in both modes to simplify processing and will default to a quality mode.


**Structuring API Integration**

I'm now integrating the API key selection flow, as required for `gemini-3-pro-image-preview`, and possibly the app as a whole. I'll add a UI element to trigger `window.aistudio.openSelectKey()`, ensuring users can connect their Google Cloud accounts. The `gemini.ts` service will manage this selection, ensuring the necessary API key is used for the image generation model and, potentially, the entire application, as per guidance.


**Refining Key Integration**

I'm now integrating the API key management into the application flow. I'll use `window.aistudio.hasSelectedApiKey()` before any model calls to ensure the user has provided their key. This ensures I can use `process.env.API_KEY` with confidence, especially for `gemini-3-pro-image-preview`. This approach should guarantee the `generateImage` service uses the correct API key. I am now adding file descriptions and structure and will be implementing this architecture.


**Enforcing the Schema**

I'm solidifying the JSON response schema for Gemini, guaranteeing a consistent format for story, inventory, quest, visual prompts, and choices. This will enable streamlined processing and easier integration with the UI, ensuring smooth game progression and a user-friendly experience.

**Debugging Microphone Mute**

I'm currently investigating a bug with the `LiveSession` component. The user reported that muting the microphone through the UI doesn't stop audio transmission to the Gemini Live API. **Pinpointing the State Bug**

I've homed in on the `LiveSession` component, specifically `onaudioprocess`. It appears the bug stems from a closure issue with `isMicOn`. The `onaudioprocess` handler captures the initial value of `isMicOn`, and doesn't update with state changes. I now see the solution - either refactor to use a ref to make the value dynamically accessible, or find a way for the event listener to be updated with the state.


**Resolving the Mic Issue**

I've determined that `useRef` is the appropriate fix, given the closure scope issue with `isMicOn` in `onaudioprocess`.  Updating `toggleMic` to manage both state and the ref, and modifying the callback to access the ref's current value, should resolve the audio transmission problem. I'm also looking at the `useEffect` dependencies, and it confirms the ref approach is the cleanest fix to ensure `onaudioprocess` always has access to the mic mute state.  Now I'm ready to update the code.


**Finalizing the Mic Fix**

I've implemented the `useRef` solution and verified that audio transmission stops when the microphone is muted via the UI. The initial state and ref initialization with `true` aligns with the desired default behavior. I've confirmed that the microphone state is correctly managed during session startup, within the `startSession` function and its processor setup. I've ensured `onaudioprocess` now correctly references the `isMicOnRef.current` value.


**Refining the Mic Logic**

I've verified the `useRef` implementation successfully addresses the audio transmission issue. I've considered that, while disconnecting the node on mute is technically an option, keeping the processor active for rapid unmuting provides a better user experience. Additionally, the initialization of `isMicOn` to `true` during component mount and `isOpen` change is also important to consider. I'm ensuring `isMicOnRef.current` stays synchronized with the `isMicOn` state to maintain consistency.


**Ensuring State Consistency**

I've added the `useEffect` hook to synchronize the `isMicOnRef` ref with the `isMicOn` state on every re-render. This addresses potential stale state issues, particularly when the component re-renders for reasons other than `toggleMic`. The `startSession` function now correctly accesses the up-to-date `isMicOnRef.current` value in `onaudioprocess`, making this the final fix. I've also reviewed the component and ensured that all relevant logic uses the ref for microphone state.


**Analyzing Visualizer Synchronization**

I'm now focusing on the visualizer's behavior and potential state inconsistencies. The user's feedback, "Ensure the microphone is muted in voice mode," solidified that `onaudioprocess` is crucial. The fact that the visualizer could still be showing activity even when muted indicates a possible synchronization issue between the visualizer's state and the `isMicOn` flag. I'm investigating how the `visualize` function and its internal `draw` function are called within `startSession` and `requestAnimationFrame`. The `draw` loop's dependency on the component's scope and `isMicOn` in the context of the session start is a key area of focus for me. **Fixing Visualizer Sync**

I'll add the necessary ref and sync the `useEffect`, keeping `toggleMic` as is. The change to `components/LiveSession.tsx` looks simple.


**Confirming the Visualizer Fix**

I've updated `components/LiveSession.tsx` and verified the visualizer now correctly reflects the microphone's mute state.  The `isMicOnRef.current` usage in both the `onaudioprocess` handler and the `draw` function ensures state consistency and resolves the synchronization issue. **Updating LiveSession Logic**

I'm now diving into the idea of world events to avoid a static feeling. I'm aiming for "Eras" that tweak the rules, creating FOMO and immersion. Given the constraints, this will have to be simulated locally or pseudo-randomly.  I think this will add the dynamism the game needs.

I've been exploring a range of ideas to deepen the player experience.  "Mystery & Awe" introduces artifacts with concealed properties, stimulating curiosity, though it might be UI-intensive.  I'm also considering a "Cursed Item System" that embraces the roguelite vibe with risky, high-reward items. I'm also considering ways to deepen companion relationships by introducing "Bond Levels".

I'm structuring the game with a clear endgame: an ascension system.  A run will be segmented into acts with increasing difficulty culminating in a final boss encounter. I'm also planning the new endgame with a complete set of features.

I've been working on a more dynamic endpoint to the game. When `isBossFight` triggers, the UI will change, and after the Act 3 Boss, a gold and blue "YOU ASCENDED" victory screen will show. These will signal a successful run. This should give the player something to work towards.

Finally, I'm integrating visual flair to indicate boss fights and handling victory and ascension resets within the `App.tsx` file.

I'm now implementing the UI changes for boss fights: aggressive text box borders and "Battle Music" when `isBossFight` is active.  I'm also working on displaying the Ascension Tier in the Class Selection/Pantheon screen.  I believe this will enhance the core gameplay loop significantly, providing clearer goals and progress indicators.  I've also started working on the `actProgress` bar for Act Progression.

To make "Infinity Quest" the "best game in the world," we need to complete the Core Game Loop. Currently, the game is endless and lacks a defined "Win Condition." Psychological research shows that **Goal Gradient** (seeing the finish line) and **Mastery** (beating difficult challenges) are key to retention.

1.  **Act Structure (The Journey):**
